coredata.dc:title: Resistive switching characteristics of ZnO–graphene quantum dots and their use as an active component of an organic memory cell with one diode-one resistor architecture
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Ji, Yongsung
Lee, Sang-A
Cha, An-Na
Goh, Munju
Bae, Sukang
Lee, Sanghyun
Son, Dong Ick
Kim, Tae-Wook
coredata.dc:description:
We investigated the resistive switching characteristics of a polystyrene:ZnO–graphene quantum dots system and its potential application in a one diode-one resistor architecture of an organic memory cell. The log–log I–V plot and the temperature-variable I–V measurements revealed that the switching mechanism in a low-current state is closely related to thermally activated transport. The turn-on process was induced by a space-charge-limited current mechanism resulted from the ZnO–graphene quantum dots acting as charge trap sites, and charge transfer through filamentary path. The memory device with a diode presented a ∼103
I ON/I OFF ratio, stable endurance cycles (102 cycles) and retention times (104 s), and uniform cell-to-cell switching. The one diode-one resistor architecture can effectively reduce cross-talk issue and realize a cross bar array as large as ∼3kbit in the readout margin estimation. Furthermore, a specific word was encoded using the standard ASCII character code.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nonvolatile memory
Organic resistive memory
ZnO–Graphene quantum dot
One diode-one resistor architecture

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The synthesis of ZnO–graphene quasi core–shell structure QDs
Fabrication of ZnO–G QDs based 1D-1R device
Electrical characteristic and charge transport mechanism

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic of as-fabricated1D-1R device. (b) Cross-sectional TEM image of 1D-1R device. (c) HRTEM image of synthesized ZnO–G QDs.
XPS spectra of the ZnO–Graphene QDs. (a) C 1s and (b) O 1s peaks.
(a) I–V characteristic of memory-only (1R) device. (b) Log–log plot of I–V characteristics at 300K.
Arrhenius plot of ON and OFF current states at temperature range from 80 to 300K.
(a) I–V characteristic of diode-only (1D) device. (b) I–V characteristic of diode integrated device onto memory-only device (1D-1R).

(a) Equivalent circuit of a N×N 1D-1R cross-bar array in the worst case read scenario. (b) Normalized readout margin as a function of the number of word/bit lines in a N×N cross-bar array for 1R and 1D-1R device.

Programmed current state and current path (a) 1R device and (b) 1D-1R device. (c) Histogram of current states of 1R and 1D-1R device during read process. (d) Currents histogram after reading process exhibiting letter of ‘SIMRC’ by standard ASCII character code.
(a) Endurance cycles obtained by repetitive switching, (b) retention characteristics and (c) cumulative probability of 1D-1R device.

head.ce:title: Resistive switching characteristics of ZnO–graphene quantum dots and their use as an active component of an organic memory cell with one diode-one resistor architecture

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Soft Innovative Materials Research Center, Institute of Advanced Composite Materials, Korea Institute of Science and Technology, Joellabuk-do 565-905, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Korea Institute of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Polymer-Nano Science and Technology, Chonbuk National University, 664-14 Duckjin-dong, Duckjin-gu, Jeonju 561-756, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Polymer-Nano Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Current Address: Department of Chemistry, Rice University, 6100 Main Street, Houston, TX 77005, USA

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
We investigated the resistive switching characteristics of a polystyrene:ZnO–graphene quantum dots system and its potential application in a one diode-one resistor architecture of an organic memory cell. The log–log I–V plot and the temperature-variable I–V measurements revealed that the switching mechanism in a low-current state is closely related to thermally activated transport. The turn-on process was induced by a space-charge-limited current mechanism resulted from the ZnO–graphene quantum dots acting as charge trap sites, and charge transfer through filamentary path. The memory device with a diode presented a ∼103
I ON/I OFF ratio, stable endurance cycles (102 cycles) and retention times (104 s), and uniform cell-to-cell switching. The one diode-one resistor architecture can effectively reduce cross-talk issue and realize a cross bar array as large as ∼3kbit in the readout margin estimation. Furthermore, a specific word was encoded using the standard ASCII character code.

ce:section.ce:para: Nonvolatile organic resistive memory devices have received great attention due to their light weight, material variety, easy fabrication process, flexibility, and solution processability [1–7]. In particular, nonvolatile organic resistive memory devices are good candidates for next generation memory devices due to their solution processability because a solution process allows for any organic memory device to be fabricated with inkjet or roll-to-roll printing methods on flexible or rigid substrates [1,2,7].

Recently, research on organic nonvolatile resistive memory devices has had remarkable progress. In particular, several research groups have presented reports discussing printing methods and have demonstrated flexible organic memory devices [2,4,6–8]. Song et al. and Kwan et al. developed a multi-layer stacked memory cell array using a cross-bar architecture [9,10]. Although the cross-bar structure is ideal for integrating organic memory cells in a limited area, cross-talk between neighboring cells has presented a significant obstacle to the development of nonvolatile organic resistive memory devices into highly integrated circuit for decades. This obstacle has been overcome by combining a memory element with various switch elements (diodes or transistors) which can control the direction of current flow in a cross-bar architecture [6,11–13]. However, fundamental research on the materials and operation mechanisms are still necessary for successful development for nonvolatile organic resistive memory devices.

For decades, research on active materials for nonvolatile organic resistive memory devices has been proposed blending systems that combine insulating polymers with metallic or semiconducting particles [14–22]. So far, however, there have been negligible numbers of reports on blending systems composed of an organic matrix with nanoparticles that have a semiconducting core and a graphene outer shell.

Graphene has been extensively studied as a potential candidate for use in flexible and transparent electrodes due to its outstanding conductivity, flexibility, and transparency [8,23–26]. However, it has been very difficult to use graphene itself in electronic devices, except for transparent electrodes, due to the absence of a band gap [27,28]. On the other hand, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are of great interest as a result of their band gap engineering and their zero-dimensional property because GQDs have a strong quantum confinement effect and edge effect when their sizes are below 10nm [27,29]. As a result of these properties, ZnO–graphene quantum dots (ZnO–G QDs) were introduced as active components for optoelectronic devices due to their excellent quantum confinement effect, band gap, and interface engineering [27]. This quantum confinement effect can trap charges and induce resistive switching phenomena in organic memory devices [28].

In this work, we introduce novel ZnO–G QDs and demonstrate a solution process to fabricate a nonvolatile organic resistive memory cell array with a one diode-one resistor (1D-1R) architecture. The memory layer and the diode layer were patterned by exposing a blended photoactive crosslinker (bis-perfluorobenzoazide: Bis-FB-N3) to 254nm ultraviolet (UV) light through a shadow mask using conventional lithography. The charge transport of the memory device was closely related to thermally activated transport before the turn-on process. The turn-on of the devices was then induced with a space-charge-limited current (SCLC), and after the turn-on process, the trapped-charge-limited current (TCLC) mechanism and the current flow were related to charge transfer through filamentary path. The 1D-1R cell showed typical unipolar switching of only a positive polarity with a ∼103
I ON/I OFF ratio and stable endurance cycles (102 cycles) and retention times (104 s). The high rectification ratio between reverse bias and forward biases enabled the realization of ∼3kbit cross-bar memory with an at least 10% readout margin estimation. Also, the abbreviation of the name of our center was encoded using the standard ASCII character code.

Graphite powder (Alfa Aesar, 5g) was ultrasonicated with 120ml of a 1:3 mixture of HNO3 (17M) and H2SO4 (18M) for 2h at a power level of 200W, while maintaining the temperature at 45°C [30]. The dispersion was maintained at room temperature for 4days and during which time the color of the dispersion becomes charcoal color. The dispersion was then washed repeatedly with water by centrifugation and decantation, and finally washed with ethanol. The product was allowed to dry at 55°C for 12h. The final graphite oxide material had a grayish appearance and was not as shiny as the starting material [27].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The synthesis of ZnO–graphene quasi core–shell structure QDs
ce:section.ce:para: A 40mg of graphite oxide was uniformly dispersed in 40ml of dimethyl formamide (DMF) through ultrasonication for 10min. A 0.92g of Zinc acetate dihydrate [Zn(CH3COO)2⋅2H2O] was dissolved in 200ml of DMF, then the graphite oxide solution was added with continual stirring to form a stable precursor. Subsequently, the mixed solution was heated to 95°C and was maintained at that temperature for 5h. Then the color of ZnO–graphene QDs powder changes to white–grayish color. It was subjected to repeated washing with ethanol by centrifugation, and finally with water. The final ZnO–graphene QDs powder was obtained after drying the product at 55°C [27].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of ZnO–G QDs based 1D-1R device
ce:section.ce:para: A SiO2 (300nm)/Si substrate (15.3mm×15.3mm) was cleaned with acetone and isopropyl alcohol using an ultrasonic cleaning process. For the bottom electrodes of the memory cell array, Al was deposited on the cleaned substrate in four lines with an 800-μm line-width pattern using a shadow mask by a thermal evaporator with a deposition rate of 5Å/s at a pressure of ∼10−7 torr.

For the UV-patternable organic memory cell, polystyrene (PS; 7mg) and ZnO–G QDs (0.5mg) were dissolved into chlorobenzene (1ml) with Bis-FB-N3 (5mg). To improve the film quality, the Al electrode-deposited substrate was UV-ozone treated for 10min [31]. The prepared solution was spin-coated onto the substrate at 2000rpm for 90s after being filtered through a 0.2μm polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) filter. In order to pattern the memory layer, the spin-coated film on the substrate covered with shadow mask and was exposed to UV (254nm) light for 5min, creating pattern sizes of each cell of 1700μm×2500μm. After UV exposure, the substrate was sufficiently rinsed using chlorobenzene, spinning at 4000rpm, and baking on a hot plate at 90°C for 10min, an then the middle Al electrodes were deposited onto a patterned memory active layer with a shadow mask where the pattern size of each cell was 2000μm×2000μm.

For the UV-patternable organic schottky diode cell, P3HT (50mg) with Bis-FB-N3 (5mg) were dissolved in chlorobenzene (1ml) and were spin-coated onto a prepared substrate at 1000rpm for 90s. To pattern the diode cell, the spin-coated P3HT film was exposed to UV (254nm) for 5min after being covered with a shadow mask with a pattern size of each cell of 2500μm×1700μm. All UV crosslinking and patterning processes were performed in an N2-filled glove box. For the top electrode of the diodes, Au was deposited with a shadow mask with a pattern size of each cell of 2940μm×1400μm. Finally, Al was deposited on the Au electrodes of the diode cells to make the 1D-1R cell array. All electrical characteristics of the 1D-1R memory cell array were measured using both Keithley 4200-SCS and HP4145B semiconductor parameter analyzer in an N2-filled glove box.

Fig. 1 a shows the illustrated schematics of the vertically stacked 4×4 1D-1R memory cell array (Al/Au/P3HT/Al/PS:ZnO–G QDs/Al/SiO2/Si). Typically, an organic device structure with more than two layers is difficult to fabricate and shows poor device performance as a result of the erosion that occurs when processing each organic layer. Due to both of these issues that affect the processibility and the integration of organic materials, a photosensitive crosslinker (ie. bis-perfluorobenzoazide: Bis-FB-N3) was used to pattern each discrete active organic layer [6,32,33].

Fig. 1b shows a cross-sectional transmission electron microscope (TEM) image of the 1D-1R memory cell where the thicknesses of the memory (PS:ZnO–G QDs) layer and the diode (P3HT) were measured to be ∼20nm and ∼700nm, respectively. High-resolution TEM showed that the outer shell of the ZnO-core quantum dot was of almost a single graphene layer (Fig. 1c). The interplanar spacing in the crystalline petal was of 0.26nm, which is the distance between two (002) planes of the hexagonal ZnO phase, exhibiting preferential growth along the [002] direction (c-axes). In order to characterize the properties of the synthesized ZnO–G QDs, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was carried out. Fig. 2 shows XPS spectra of typical ZnO–G QDs on p-Si (100) substrates. As shown in Fig. 2a, the C 1s XPS spectrum of ZnO–G QDs has five different peaks centered at 284.5 (sp2C), 285.5 (sp3C), 286.4 (CO), 287.5 (CO), 289.1 (COO groups), and 290.3eV (π–π∗ satellite peak). The O 1s peak is asymmetric, which indicates the presence of at least four oxygen species, as shown in Fig. 2b. The O 1s peak at 531.2eV in the XPS spectra is attributed to oxide ions in the ZnO crystal lattice (ZnO bonds). The main peak at 531eV is dominantly related to the oxygen of the ZnO in the ZnO–G QDs, as shown in Fig. 2b.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrical characteristic and charge transport mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 3 a shows the current–voltage (I–V) characteristics of the memory only (Al/PS:ZnO–G QDs/Al, 1R cell). In order to make on current state, we applied bias from 0–5V to 5–0V in dual sweep mode (1st sweep). As shown in Fig. 3a, when we applied a voltage on the memory cell, it showed a very low current value (high resistance state, HRS). With a higher bias, the current flow started to increase and turned to a high current state at over 2.5V (sweep from 0 to 5V). The memory cells kept their high current state (low resistance state, LRS) during a reverse bias sweep from 5V to 0V, indicating the nonvolatile characteristics of our memory cell. After the writing process (double sweep from 0V to 5V), the memory cell was reset to the initial current state by applying bias from 0 to 10V or even more, showing typical unipolar characteristic (2nd sweep). Unipolar type memory device can be turned on and off in same polarity, and has symmetric switching characteristics as shown as 3rd and 4th sweep in Fig. 3a. Because of this property, unipolar memory device could be operated like bipolar type. The bipolar type memory device should be turned on and off in different polarity, and bipolar type memory device could not be operated like unipolar type memory device [34]. Bipolar type memory device show better reproducibility compared with unipolar type memory device but bipolar type is not suitable for the 1D-1R architecture because it requires both polarities in operation.

There were several reports on ZnO nanoparticle based memory devices. They showed good memory performance but they had only bipolar switching characteristics [35–38]. In addition, Some of them requires additional transport layer for charge transfer [35–37]. However, ZnO–G QDs based memory device showed outstanding charge trapping and transport characteristics without additional ETL layer, rewritable characteristics and ∼103 of I ON/I OFF ratio at 1V. In order to study the origin of the resistive switching of the PS:ZnO–G QDs memory cell, a log–log plot of the I–V analysis was carried out, as shown in Fig. 3b. At a low voltage regime (1), the current was almost linearly proportional to the applied voltage (α1 ∼1.2), which can be explained by the thermally-generated free carriers [3,39,40]. After this regime, the slope of the log–log plot became steeper [α2 ∼2.4, regime (2)] indicating that the switching was related to the space-charge-limited current (SCLC) [1,3,18,39,40]. In this case, the ZnO–G QDs acted as charge carrier trap media, and the current flow was governed by the Mott–Gurney law as
J =
9 ε
ε
0
μ Θ
V
2
d
3
, where ε is the dielectric constant of the polymer, ε 0 is the permittivity of free space, and Θ is the trapping fraction [18,40,41]. When the applied bias was further increased, the log–log plot of the I–V characteristics exhibited a large slope of over α3 ∼9.5 at regime (3), as shown in Fig. 3b. This strong SCLC is known as a trapped-charge-limited current (TCLC), and substantial charge trapping happens in this regime. In order to estimate trapped charge density, we fabricated organic memory transistor with Au (source and drain electrodes)/pentacene (channel layer)/PS:ZnO–G QDs (charge trap layer)/SiO2 (100nm-thick, dielectric layer)/heavily doped p-type Si (bottom gate) structure. The trapped charge density was calculated by the following equation
n =
| Δ
V
th
| ×
C
i
e
, where n is trapped charge density per unit area at PS:ZnO–G QDs, ΔV th is threshold voltage shift (25.6V), C i is capacitance per unit area (25.9nF/cm2), and e is elementary electric charge (1.602×10−19 C). The estimated trapped charge density was found to be ∼4.14×1012/cm2 (See Supporting Information). After this regime, the memory device turned on [region (4) in Fig. 3b], and the slope α4 ∼1.3 indicated ohmic behavior [39]. In this region the ZnO–G QDs traps were completely filled and the charge carriers easily passed through the active layer [1,3]. Once the trap is completely filled, a carbon rich filamentary path could be generated with in active layer (In the analysis of energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy after tuned on, there was no metallic component except carbon in active layer. Data is not shown here) [1]. The ohmic behavior after turn on process is a proof of carbon rich filamentary path. In order to turn off the memory device, the filamentary path should be ruptured by Joule heating and the memory device was turned off after applied high bias as shown in Fig. 3a.

For more intensive study of the switching behavior, the charge transport was analyzed through temperature-variable I–V measurements in a vacuum pressure of ∼10−5 torr. Fig. 4 shows the Arrhenius plot of the I ON and I OFF state in a temperature range from 80K to 300K. The I ON and I OFF were read at 1.5V. As shown in Fig. 4, the I OFF characteristics could be explained by the thermally activated transport with activation energy (E a) of ∼28.7meV over certain temperature (∼150K) [16]. This temperature dependent charge transport corresponds to the slope of region (1) in Fig. 3b. On the other hand, the I ON characteristics showed almost temperature-independent behavior with negligible activation energy (∼1.7meV) in the temperature range from 80K to 300K. This implies that the I ON current is mainly attributed to charge transfer through filamentary path in the high electric field region.

For a more practical application of PS:ZnO–G QDs, we fabricated a memory device with a one diode-one resistor (1D-1R) architecture in order to avoid the misreading issue between neighboring cells generated by cross-talk [5]. Fig. 5 a shows the I–V characteristics of the diode only (Al/Au/P3HT/Al, 1D cell). The current increased as the positively applied bias increased due to the difference between the work function of Au (5.1eV) and the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) level (4.9eV), which makes an ohmic contact. However, the current did not increased as the negatively applied bias increased as a result of the barrier between the work function of Al (4.1eV) and the HOMO level of P3HT (4.9eV), which makes a Schottky barrier. Fig. 5b shows the I–V characteristic of the vertically stacked diode onto memory device, which creates the 1D-1R structure. Unlike Fig. 3a (1R), the 1D-1R shows memory switching characteristics only in the positive bias region. Since the vertically stacked diode device controls the switching characteristics of the memory device, a low-current state is kept even though the negatively applied bias increased, as shown in Fig. 5b.

In order to estimate the enhancement of 1D-1R structure compared to 1R structure in cross-bar array, the readout margin of 1R and 1D-1R were characterized as shown in Fig. 6 . For estimation of the maximum number of word/bit lines in the worst case scenario assumption, one bit-line pull-up (One BLPU) scheme which can be depicted as the equivalent N×N cross-bar array is considered as shown in Fig. 6a [42–45]. All unselected cells are assumed in LRS with negligible line resistance for the maximum sneak current (caused by cross-talk issue) value through unselected cells (purple line in Fig. 6a), which could cause read error when selected cell is in HRS. The difference between LRS and HRS in the selected cell could be distinguished based on the voltage drop (ΔV out) difference through the pull-up resistor (R pu), which is dependent on both the selected current across the selected cell (red line in Fig. 6a) and sneak current across the unselected cells (purple line in Fig. 6b) when the read voltage is applied to the selected cell. The R 2 at unselected cell can almost decide the total resistance of sneak current due to its reversed biased diode in 1D-1R cross-bar array structure, and the R 1 and R 3 could be excluded in the readout margin calculation [44–45]. The forward resistance of a 1D-1R cell in LRS and HRS at positive read voltage is defined as
R
LRS
F
and
R
HRS
F
and the reverse resistance in LRS at negative read voltage is defined as
R
LRS
R
(Fig. 6a). The readout margin normalized to pull-up voltage (ΔV out/V pu) can be calculated using the resistor voltage divider equation [44,45].
(1)
Δ
V
out
V
pu
=
R
pu
R
LRS
F
| |
R
LRS
R
( N - 1 )
2
+
R
pu
-
R
pu
R
HRS
F
| |
R
LRS
R
( N - 1 )
2
+
R
pu
.

The pull-up resistance (R pu) was set to
R
LRS
F
at read voltage (1V) in order to obtain the maximum readout margin. The calculated readout margin of 1R and 1D-1R cross-bar structure are shown in Fig. 6b. The calculated maximum allowed word/bit lines in the cross-bar array increased from 3 (9bit) for 1R device to 54 (∼3kbit) for 1D-1R device in the 10% readout margin.

Furthermore an addressing test was carried out on both the 1R device and the 1D-1R device, as shown in Fig. 7 . In the 1R device, to verify the cross-talk issue, one cell was made in the OFF current state (designated as “0”), and the OFF current state was isolated with three ON states (designated as “1”), as shown as Fig. 7a. Due to cross-talk in the 1R device, the OFF current state could not be read correctly, and every cell was read as being in the ON current state, as shown in the left column of Fig. 7c.

In order to verify the effect of the integrated diode, the same experiment was carried out for the 1D-1R device, as shown in Fig. 7b. Unlike the 1R device, the 1D-1R device showed exact addressing as a result of the integrated diode, and the OFF current state was read correctly, as shown in the right column of Fig. 7c. Using the precise addressing test, the abbreviation of the name of our research center (Soft Innovative Material Research Center, SIMRC) was written using the ON current state (1) and the OFF current state (0) with standard ASCII character codes, as shown in Fig. 7d. Each letter was written in 2×4 cells within the 4×4 cell array in the 1D-1R device after refreshing.

In order to verify the operational reliability of the device, an endurance test was carried out for 102 cycles, as shown in Fig. 8 a. The device shows little fluctuation, but the operational failure (overlap between ON and OFF current state) was not observed during the 102 cycles. Also, a retention test was carried out, as shown in Fig. 8b. The ON current state, OFF current state, and diode leakage current were well maintained for 104 s. In order to identify the operational uniformity of each cell of the 16 cells, a cell-to-cell uniformity analysis was carried out, as shown in Fig. 8c, for practical memory application. As shown in Fig. 8c, the cumulative probability of Al/Au/P3HT/Al/PS:ZnO–G QDs/Al device showed a narrow distribution in both the ON and OFF current states.

We introduced novel ZnO–G QDs as active components and demonstrated a solution-processed organic nonvolatile resistive memory cell array with one diode-one resistor (1D-1R) architecture. The switching mechanism of the ZnO–G QDs memory device was governed by thermally activated transport before the turn-on process. The turn-on process was induced by a space-charge-limited current (SCLC), and the trapped-charge-limited current (TCLC) mechanism and current flow were related to transfer thorough filamentary path after turn-on. The 1D-1R cell showed typical unipolar switching with a ∼103
I ON/I OFF ratio and stable endurance cycles (102 cycles) and retention times (104 s) for only the positive polarity. Undesired cross-talk between neighboring cells was solved using the 1D-1R architecture and the cross-bar array could be increased up to ∼3kbit in readout margin estimation. Furthermore, a specific word was encoded using the standard ASCII character code.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Korea Institute of Science and Technology (KIST) Institutional Program (2Z04420).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.orgel.2015.01.010.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: IEEE Int. Electron. Dev. Meet. Tech. Dig.
IEEE Int. Electron. Dev. Meet. Tech. Dig.
